attributes.stem:
An 8-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent with a three-day history of left hip pain and an associated limp. There is no history of trauma. He is otherwise in good health, apart from an upper respiratory tract infection two weeks ago. He mentions that he can mobilise without pain with simple analgesia including paracetamol and NSAIDs. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Examination of the left hip reveals irritability with all movements. There is no redness, increased warmth or tenderness on palpation over the hip. Gait examination reveals that he is able to weight bear on both limbs and mobilises with an antalgic gait. Femoral, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable bilaterally.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line investigation at this presentation?
attributes.explanation:
This presentation is consistent with transient synovitis, the most common cause of acute hip pain in children aged 3-10 years. It often follows a recent mild viral infection such as an upper respiratory tract infection. Children with transient synovitis typically present with hip pain that may radiate to the knee, and an antalgic gait. Symptoms usually resolve within 7 days and can be adequately managed with simple analgesia such as paracetamol, as in this case. In the absence of red flags on history and examination (such as trauma, inability to weight-bear, nocturnal pain, or systemic symptoms), no further investigations are needed and the child can be safely discharged home with symptomatic management. Notably however, children considered likely to have transient synovitis that are discharged without investigation should be appropriately safety netted and parents informed that pain persisting beyond seven days should prompt review and may warrant further investigation.

Septic arthritis is an important differential diagnosis to consider. However, these patients are typically febrile, systemically unwell, and have severe pain with minimal movement of the affected joint. They often lie with the hip fixed in abduction and external rotation to minimise pain. Suspected septic arthritis requires urgent orthopaedic referral for arthrocentesis and consideration of joint washout.

Hip x-ray and ultrasound may be indicated in some cases of paediatric hip pain to exclude fracture or detect joint effusion. However, they are not routinely required in uncomplicated cases of transient synovitis without red flags.

An MRI scan provides excellent visualisation of the joint and surrounding soft tissues. It may be considered if the diagnosis is unclear or symptoms are atypical, but is not a first-line investigation for transient synovitis.

Rheumatoid factor and inflammatory markers are useful in the evaluation of inflammatory arthritides such as juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA). However, JIA usually presents with morning joint stiffness, intermittent fever, and symptoms persisting for at least 6 weeks. Patients with osteomyelitis are typically systemically unwell with high fever and localised signs of inflammation, but retain some degree of joint movement.

Perthes disease (Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease) is an important differential diagnosis to consider if hip pain persists beyond 1-2 weeks. This condition involves avascular necrosis of the femoral head. Importantly, radiographic changes in Perthes disease may not be evident for 2-6 weeks after symptom onset. Therefore, if this child's symptoms persist beyond the expected timeframe for transient synovitis, further investigation with imaging would be warranted, recognising that initial x-rays may be normal despite the presence of early Perthes disease. In cases of high clinical suspicion with normal radiographs, MRI may detect early changes of avascular necrosis before they are visible on plain films.
learningPoints: Symptoms of transient synovitis usually resolve within seven days with appropriate simple analgesia.
Transient synovitis is a clinical diagnosis, and further investigation is only necessary if red flags are present.
Perthes disease should be considered if hip pain persists beyond 1-2 weeks in a child, though radiographic changes may not be evident for 2-6 weeks after symptom onset.
references: Ryan DD. Differentiating Transient Synovitis of the Hip from More Urgent Conditions. Pediatr Ann 2016; 45:209-13. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27294495/
Boros C, Whitehead B. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Australian family physician. 2010 Sep;39(9). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2010/september/juvenile-idiopathic-arthritis
Wall C, Donnan L. Septic arthritis in children. Australian Family Physician. 2015 Apr;44(4):213-5. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2015/april/septic-arthritis-in-children

RCH. The limping or non-weight bearing child. Clinical Guidelines Royal Children Hospital website. July 2020. Viewed April 2023. Available via: https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Child_with_limp/

Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease: diagnosis, imaging and classifications. In: Radiology Key [online]. Accessed 2025 Jun 25. Available at: https://radiologykey.com/legg-calve-perthes-disease-diagnosis-imaging-and-classifications/
